coredata.dc:title: Fluorometric detection of cholesterol based on β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots via competitive host-guest recognition
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Qian
Fang, Siying
Fang, Yafen
Qian, Zhaosheng
Feng, Hui
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive and selective fluorescent method for detection of cholesterol based on β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQD) nanoprobe through competitive host–guest recognition has been developed. The bright fluorescence of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe can be effectively quenched by the introduction of a very small amount of p-nitrophenol, and strong quenching ability of p-nitrophenol to the probe was identified. This efficient fluorescence quenching as a static quenching is due to the formation of nonfluorescent complex between β-CD-CQD and p-nitrophenol induced by host-guest interaction between them. Cholesterol, as a more suited guest molecule of β-cyclodextrin, can form an inclusion complex with β-cyclodextrin with a much greater binding constant than p-nitrophenol. As a result, the presence of cholesterol causes a replacement of guest molecule of β-CD moiety in the probe through a competitive way, and the inclusion of cholesterol in the probe as the removal of p-nitropheonl induces significant fluorescence enhancement. The fluorescence recovery can be achieved by continuous addition of cholesterol. In terms of this relationship between enhanced fluorescence and concentration of cholesterol, a quantitative measurement for cholesterol based on β-CD-CQD nanoprobe has been established. Under the optimized detection conditions, this method shows good analytical performance in detection of cholesterol with lowest detection limit of 0.7±0.1µM and a relatively wide linear scope of 110µM. Specificity test and complex matrix test show that this method possesses excellent selectivity to cholesterol, and complex matrix such as serum does not exert apparent influence on the analytical performance, which enables its use in practical serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Cholesterol
Fluorometric detection
Host-guest recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of 6-aminoethylamino-6-deoxy-β-cyclodextrin modified carbon quantum dot (β-CD-CQD) nanoprobe
Fluorescence quenching of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe by p-nitrophenol
Fluorometric detection of cholesterol based on β-CD-CQD nanoprobe
Synthesis and characterization of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe
Fluorescence quenching of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe induced by p-nitrophenol
Fluorescence turn-on of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe through a competitive replacement
Fluorometric detection of cholesterol based on β-CD-CQD probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TME image, (B) size distribution in diameter and (C) wide XPS spectrum of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe, and comparison in FT-IR spectra between mere CQDs and β-CD-CQD nanoprobe.
(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe and CQDs. (B) Time-resolved decay curves and fitting curves of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe and CQDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe as a function of concentration of p-nitrophenol in the range of 0.0–301.0µM. (B) The plot of fluorescence intensity versus the concentration of p-nitrophenol in the range of 2.0–36.0µM. (C) The plot of the values for I0/I versus concentration of p-nitrophenol in the range of 0.0–36.0µM. (D) Lifetimes of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe in the presence of different amounts of p-nitrophenol.

Changes of fluorescence spectra of β-CD-CQD in the presence of different constituents: (a) mere β-CD-CQD probe; (b) in the presence of p-nitrophenol (130.0µM); (c) in the presence of p-nitrophenol (130.0µM) and cholesterol (130.0µM).

(A) Change of fluorescence spectra of β-CD-CQD with 130.0µM p-nitrophenol versus the concentration of cholesterol in the range of 0.0–170.0µM using PBS buffer (pH 7.4) containing 20% ethanol. (B) The plot of fitting curve between fluorescence intensity and concentration of cholesterol in the range of 2.8–110.0µM.
Illustration of detection strategy of cholesterol detection using β-CD-CQD nanoprobe via a competitive host–guest interaction.
Data for standard addition experiment based on as-established detection method for cholesterol using 50-fold dilution of calf serum as the complex matrix.

row.entry: Sum of added and original cholesterol (μM)

head.ce:title: Fluorometric detection of cholesterol based on β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots via competitive host-guest recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive and selective fluorescent method for detection of cholesterol based on β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQD) nanoprobe through competitive host–guest recognition has been developed. The bright fluorescence of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe can be effectively quenched by the introduction of a very small amount of p-nitrophenol, and strong quenching ability of p-nitrophenol to the probe was identified. This efficient fluorescence quenching as a static quenching is due to the formation of nonfluorescent complex between β-CD-CQD and p-nitrophenol induced by host-guest interaction between them. Cholesterol, as a more suited guest molecule of β-cyclodextrin, can form an inclusion complex with β-cyclodextrin with a much greater binding constant than p-nitrophenol. As a result, the presence of cholesterol causes a replacement of guest molecule of β-CD moiety in the probe through a competitive way, and the inclusion of cholesterol in the probe as the removal of p-nitropheonl induces significant fluorescence enhancement. The fluorescence recovery can be achieved by continuous addition of cholesterol. In terms of this relationship between enhanced fluorescence and concentration of cholesterol, a quantitative measurement for cholesterol based on β-CD-CQD nanoprobe has been established. Under the optimized detection conditions, this method shows good analytical performance in detection of cholesterol with lowest detection limit of 0.7±0.1µM and a relatively wide linear scope of 110µM. Specificity test and complex matrix test show that this method possesses excellent selectivity to cholesterol, and complex matrix such as serum does not exert apparent influence on the analytical performance, which enables its use in practical serum samples.

A sensitive and selective fluorescent method for detection of cholesterol based on β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots (β-CD-CQD) nanoprobe through competitive host–guest recognition has been developed.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Cholesterol is an essential structural component of all animal cell membranes, and plays an important role in maintaining both membrane structural integrity and fluidity [1]. Cholesterol also functions as the precursor for the biosynthesis of bile acids and steroid hormones [2]. It is found that the normal level of total cholesterol in healthy human serum is generally less than 5.2mM or 200mg/dL [3]. An increased level of cholesterol in the blood, termed as hypercholesterolemia, can apparently raise the risk of vascular diseases including coronary heart diseases, hypertension and atherosclerosis [3–5]. On the other hand, the deficiency of cholesterol is suggested to be linked with appearance of depression, cancer and cerebral hemorrhage [6]. As a result, cholesterol serves an important indicator of clinical biochemistry. Up to date, a variety of analytical methods have been developed for cholesterol detection such as colorimetric [7–9], electrochemical [10–12], chromatographic [13] and fluorometric approaches [14–16]. However, most of the methods with high selectivity rely on oxidation reaction of cholesterol catalyzed by cholesterol oxidase, which is expensive and easily loses activity. Thus, it is essential to develop a detection method with high selectivity, low-cost and convenience for further improvement in detection of cholesterol.

β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD) is a cyclic polysaccharide, and is composed of seven glucose units connected by α-1,4 linkages. It has a hydrophilic outer surface and hydrophobic inner cavity. This structure provides the β-CD with a unique ability to form host-guest inclusion complexes with a wide range of suitably sized guest molecules [17]. The host-guest interaction between β-CD and cholesterol has been widely used in the selective extraction of cholesterol from different matrixes, such as food, cell membranes, blood serum, and cultured cell [18–21]. It suggests that β-CD can be an alternative of cholesterol oxidase for selective recognition of cholesterol. Carbon quantum dots have many advantages such as stable light emitting, good photostability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, and thus are being utilized to design chemo/biosensors [22–25]. On the basis of recent progress on carbon quantum dots-based biosensors for enzyme activity [26,27], herein we report fluorometric detection of cholesterol based on β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dots via a competitive host-guest recognition. Carbon quantum dots with a vast number of carboxyl groups were modified first to prepare β-cyclodextrin functionalized carbon quantum dot (β-CD-CQD) nanoprobe. As shown in Scheme 1, it is found that p-nitrophenol can enter the cavity of β-cyclodextrin moiety in the nanoprobe to form an inclusion complex, which results in fluorescence quenching of the nanoprobe. The presence of cholesterol triggers the following competitive host-guest interaction. The stronger affinity of β-cyclodextrin moiety in the probe to cholesterol than p-nitrophenol induces the replacement of the guest of β-cyclodextrin, which further causes the fluorescence recovery of the nanoprobe. The increased fluorescence is correlated with the amount of cholesterol present in the solution, and thus this correlation can be used to quantify cholesterol.

Triple-distilled water was used throughout the experimental process. Activated carbon, p-nitrophenol, and phosphate buffer saline (PBS) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cholesterol, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 6-aminoethylamino-6-deoxy-β-cyclodextrin were purchased from J&K (Beijing, China). Super newborn calf serum was bought from Sangon Biotech. company (Shanghai, China). The concentration of phosphate buffer saline (PBS) was 10.0mM. All reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of 6-aminoethylamino-6-deoxy-β-cyclodextrin modified carbon quantum dot (β-CD-CQD) nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: A brief procedure for synthesis of carbon quantum dots is described as follows according to our previous papers [27]. Activated carbon (2.0g) was added into a mixture of concentrated sulfuric acid (180mL) and nitric acid (60mL). The mixture was heated at 80°C for 5h, and then cooled and diluted with deionized water (800mL). The resulting solution was neutralized with sodium hydroxide, and further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000Da) for 3 days, and finally treated with the dialysis bag (50,000Da) to remove large non-fluorescent materials. The outside solution of the latter bag was collected and concentrated for further use.

The synthesis of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe was as follows. CQDs powder (0.05g) was dissolved in deionized water (15.0mL), and the preceding solution was adjusted to pH 4. A certain amount of EDC (0.5g) and NHS (0.5g) were added to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs for 1h. Then an excessive amount of 6-aminoethylamino-6-deoxy-β-cyclodextrin (0.05g) was added to the preceding solution. This reaction is allowed to proceed for 3 days at 80°C. Finally, as-prepared nanoprobe was purified with microporous filter (0.22μM) and a dialysis bag (1000Da) for 48h to remove molecular substances including NH2C2H4NH-β-CD, EDC and NHS. As-prepared β-CD-CQD nanoprobe was obtained and further used in the following detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe by p-nitrophenol
ce:section.ce:para: For optimization of incubation time for fluorescence quenching by p-nitrophenol, 34.0µL of p-nitrophenol solution (10.0mM) was added into 2.00mL of β-CD-CQD (0.45mg/mL) buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) in PL quartz cuvette (1cm×1cm×3cm). Then, the fluorescence of mixed solution was recorded using a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer after different incubation times of 1, 2.5, 4.5, 6.5, 8.5, 10.5 and 12.5min, respectively. Under the optimum incubation time of 2.5min, the PBS solution (2.00mL,10.0mM, pH 7.4) containing β-CD-CQD (0.45mg/mL) and various amounts of p-nitrophenol (0.0–130.0µM) was monitored by fluorescence spectroscopy at the optimal excitation wavelength. After a certain amount of p-nitrophenol is added every time, 2.5min of incubation time is required.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of cholesterol based on β-CD-CQD nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: For optimization of incubation time for fluorescence recovery by cholesterol, 15.0µL of cholesterol solution (2.00mM) in ethanol was added into 2.00mL of buffer solution containing β-CD-CQD (0.45mg/mL) and p-nitrophenol (170.0µM). Then, the fluorescence of mixed solution was recorded after different incubation times of 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 13, 16, 20, 24, 27, 32, 37, 42, 47 and 52min, respectively. Under the optimum incubation time of 42min, the mixture containing β-CD-CQD (0.45mg/mL), p-nitrophenol (130.0µM) and various amounts of cholesterol (0.0–170.0µM) in 2.00mL of buffer solution consisting of 80% PBS and 20% ethanol was monitored by fluorescence spectroscopy at the optimal excitation wavelength. After a certain amount of cholesterol is added every time, 42min of incubation time is required. For the optimization of the composition of buffer solution for detection of cholesterol, the ratios in volume between PBS solution (pH 7.4) and ethanol in the range from 100:0 to 60:40 were assessed to measure cholesterol according to the preceding procedure.

Under the optimal conditions (42min incubation and 80:20 PBS/ethanol buffer solution), the selectivity of as-established cholesterol detection method was evaluated. Ten possibly interferents, including NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, urea, glucose, glycine, tryptophan, tyrosine, ascorbic acid, and glutathione, were chosen to assess the specificity of the detection system for cholesterol. A certain amount of p-nitrophenol (130.0µM) was first added into 2.00mL of β-CD-CQD solution (0.45mg/mL). After 2.5min incubation, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were recorded as the blank. Then each of the chosen species was separately added into the above mixture, where the concentration is 90.0µM for each of them, and then the fluorescence was monitored at the excitation wavelength of 430nm after 42min incubation. Matrix effect was further assessed using 50-fold dilution of calf serum as a complex matrix. The mixtures containing β-CD-CQD (0.45mg/mL), p-nitrophenol (130.0µM) and a series of fixed amounts of cholesterol in buffer solution consisting of PBS-ethanol (80:20) and 40.0µL of calf serum were monitored by fluorescence spectrometer at the emission wavelength of 513nm after 42min incubation. The samples were spiked with different amounts of cholesterol, and their final concentrations are 0.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0 and 60.0µM, respectively.

The morphologies of all samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which was performed on a JEOL-2100F instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were conducted using a Kratos Axis ULT RAX-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a 165nm hemispherical electron energy analyzer. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. The photoluminescence spectra were conducted on a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer using a PL quartz cuvette (1cm×1cm×3cm). Time-resolved fluorescence decay tests were performed using Edinburgh Instrument Model FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer. FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet NEXUS 670 FTIR spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots have been reported in our previous paper [27], where carbon quantum dots were prepared by means of chemical oxidation of activated carbon powder, and further purified by filtration and dialysis. As-prepared CQDs serve as the starting materials to synthesize the following nanoprobe. The host molecule, 6-aminoethylamino-β-cyclodextrin, was covalently linked to the surface of CQDs via EDC/NHS coupling reaction. The nanoprobe was characterized by TEM, XPS, UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy, respectively. The TEM images in Fig. 1A indicate that β-CD-CQD nanoprobes present monodisperse state, similar to CQDs; however, size distribution of these nanoprobes in Fig. 1B are focused on 8–10nm, which are a little larger than mere CQDs in diameters partly because of relatively large size of β-cyclodextrin (around 1.5nm in diameter). XPS analysis in Fig. 1C shows that β-CD-CQD nanoprobes mainly consist of oxygen (27.0at%), carbon (63.3at%) and nitrogen (9.7at%), and a new signal at 399.1eV from N1s for β-CD-CQD probe relative to mere CQDs appears, indicating that NH2CH2CH2NH-β-CD has been covalently linked to surface of CQDs to generate β-CD-CQD probe. Comparison in FT-IR spectra between β-CD-CQD and CQDs in Fig. 1D illustrates additional absorption peaks at 2967, 1453 and 1218cm−1 derived from the vibration of N-H, C-N and NH-C˭O separately, further confirming the formation of functionalized CQDs with β-CD moiety. The fluorescence property of as-prepared β-CD-CQD was further explored. The aqueous solution of β-CD-CQD probe exhibits bright yellowish green fluorescence under a UV lamp. Fig. 2A displays the excitation and emission spectra of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe and mere CQDs, which display fluorescence emission peak at 513nm for β-CD-CQD nanoprobe when exited at its optimum wavelength of 430nm. An obvious blue-shift in emission peak is observed in large part because of the modification with amino-containing β-cyclodextrin. It has been reported that the PL blue shift induced by the chemical introduction of nitrogen or amino group into carbon quantum dots is attributed to the relatively strong electron affinity of N atoms [28]. In terms of time-resolved decay curves of β-CD-CQD and CQDs in Fig. 2B, their lifetimes are determined to be 3.3ns and 2.3ns respectively, and this appreciable difference in lifetime further denotes their distinction in fluorescence property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe induced by p-nitrophenol
ce:section.ce:para: It has been proved that β-cyclodextrin can bind with p-nitrophenol to form a stable inclusion complex, and its binding constant determined by different techniques varies from 224 to 1020 [29]. This specific host-guest interaction between them has been utilized to design various chemo/biosensors [30–32]. In this work, it is found that only a very small amount of p-nitrophenol is required to effectively quench the fluorescence of β-CD-CQD. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe was gradually quenched with the addition of p-nitrophenol, and it was attained a high quenching efficiency of 93% when the concentration of p-nitrophenol is 301.0µM. The fitting plot of fluorescence response versus p-nitrophenol level in the range from 2.0 to 36.0µM is showed in Fig. 3B. The regression equation for the calibration curve can be represented as y=−8.14x +811.2 with R2=0.995. The quenching mechanism of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe by p-nitrophenol was further explored. As showed in Fig. 3C, it can be found that there is a linear relationship between the value I0/I and the concentration of p-nitrophenol. According to Stern-Volmer equation I0/I=1+Ks[Q] =1+kqτ0[Q] where Ks, kq, and τ0 represent Stern-Volmer constant, quenching rate constant and initial lifetime respectively, kq is determined to be 4.2×1012 Lmol−1 s−1, which is several orders of magnitude than the value (109–1010 Lmol−1 s−1) for collision-controlled quenching. This great value for kq clearly illustrates the generation of ground-state complexes between β-CD-CQD and p-nitrophenol. Fig. 3D showed that the lifetime of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe in the presence of different concentrations of p-nitrophenol in the range of 0.0–36.0µM remains almost unchanged according to the fitting curves. It was further confirmed that the quenching mechanism of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe by p-nitrophenol is static quenching. Therefore, it supports that the quenching by p-nitrophenol is static quenching due to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between β-CD-CQD nanoprobe and p-nitrophenol. In addition, the quenching effect of m-nitrophenol and o-nitrophenol on the nanoprobe was also assessed. The presence of o-nitrophenol with the same amount to p-nitrophenol (301.0µM) only results in a low quenching efficiency of 40%, and almost no significant quenching is observed when the identical equivalent of m-nitrophenol co-exists with the nanoprobe. The much lower quenching efficiencies induced by o-nitrophenol or m-nitrophenol than p-nitrophenol can be ascribed to their weak interactions to β-CD moiety in the probe, indicating that good selectivity of the nanoprobe to p-nitrophenol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence turn-on of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe through a competitive replacement
ce:section.ce:para: The association constant for the complex between β-cyclodextrin and cholesterol varies from 17,000 to 34,000 depending on various pH values according to the report by Frijlink et al. [33], which is much greater than that for the complex with p-nitrophenol. This large difference in binding constant of β-cyclodextrin to cholesterol and p-nitrophenol enables us to achieve the detection of cholesterol through a competitive replacement using β-CD-CQD nanoprobe as shown in Scheme 1. The feasibility of this detection strategy was first assessed qualitatively in Fig. 4. The fluorescence of β-CD-CQD nanoprobe is sharply quenched to 25% of its initial intensity after 130.0µM p-nitrophenol is added, and then the fluorescence is almost entirely recovered after the introduction of the same amount of cholesterol to added p-nitrophenol, indicating that the presence of cholesterol causes an almost complete replacement of p-nitrophenol in β-CD moiety of the probe. An apparent blue-shift in emission peak with the addition of cholesterol with comparison to mere β-CD-CQD also supports the formation of the complex between β-CD-CQD and cholesterol. This competitive replacement strategy was also supported by the change in UV–visible spectrum of p-nitrophenol as a function of the amount of β-CD-CQD and cholesterol in Fig. S3. The formation of β-CD-CQD/p-nitrophenol complex is verified by the decrease in absorption of free p-nitrophenol in solution; The replacement of p-nitrophenol with cholesterol from β-CD moiety was confirmed by gradual increase in UV–vis absorption peaks at 402nm and 226nm of free p-nitrophenol. The feasibility of qualitative detection provides foundation for the following quantitative measurement of cholesterol based on this competitive replacement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of cholesterol based on β-CD-CQD probe
ce:section.ce:para: To quantify cholesterol in a real-time manner, detection conditions, including incubation time for fluorescence quenching by p-nitrophenol and fluorescence recovery by cholesterol, and buffer solution, were first optimized. Fig. S4 shows that only 2.5min of incubation is needed to attain the equilibrium for fluorescence quenching by p-nitrophenol while 42min of incubation is required for fluorescence recovery process, and thereby incubation times for the two processes are determined to be 2.5min and 42min for the following detection. PBS solution (pH 7.4) was first chosen as buffer solution, and the mixture containing β-CD-CQD (0.45mg/mL) and p-nitrophenol (130.0µM) in PBS solution was used as the standard assay solution. As shown in Fig. S5A, a gradual increase in fluorescence was observed as the rise of cholesterol in concentration in the assay solution from 0.0 to 130.0µM, and the fitting plot of fluorescence response versus amount of cholesterol in the range from 3.5 to 65.0µM is showed in Fig. S5B. The regression equation for the calibration curve can be represented as y=5.38x+218.00 with R2=0.991. It is found that linear scope for this method is relatively narrow when mere PBS solution was used as the buffer, which is partly because cholesterol is not insoluble in water. Thereby composition of buffer solution was optimized by adjusting the ratio of PBS solution to ethanol. When the buffer solution consisting of 90% PBS and 10% ethanol in volume was used, the dynamic and linear scopes were substantially expanded as shown in Fig. S6. The use of PBS buffer solution containing 20% ethanol gives the broadest dynamic scope of 170.0µM and the widest linear scope of 110.0µM. A similar progressive rise in fluorescence as a function of concentration of cholesterol is displayed in Fig. 5A, and the plot of fitting curve between fluorescence intensity and amount of cholesterol in Fig. 5B can be expressed as the regression equation: y=4.08x+153.05 with R2=0.9998. However, this method shows also a broad dynamic scope for detection of cholesterol when PBS solution containing 30% ethanol is used, but its sensitivity is remarkably decreased in terms of their regression equations for the plots of fitting curves (Fig. S7). When the content of ethanol is increased to 40% in assay solution, the detection system becomes very instable. As a result, the assay solution composed of 80% PBS solution and 20% ethanol is the best choice for accurate measurement of cholesterol. The detection limit is estimated to be 0.9±0.1µM using three times standard deviations according to the calibration curve. The detection limits for different assay solutions varies from 0.7±0.1 to 1.9±0.2µM, indicating that limit of detection is not significantly impacted by difference in buffer solution. The detection limit of this method is inferior to that based fluorescein [16], but is comparable to or better than that based on silver nanoclusters (0.15µM) [14] and those from colorimetric approaches [8,9]. Most importantly, this method is sensitive sufficiently to measure the concentration of cholesterol in real serum sample, which normal contains around 5.2mM for healthy people [3].

To investigate the specificity of constructed assay for cholesterol, ten possibly interfering species which are present in serum, including NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, urea, glucose, glycine, tryptophan, tyrosine, ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GSH), were assessed using standard assay solution. It is found from Fig. S8 that the separate introduction of there preceding chemicals into buffer solution (PBS: ethanol 80:20) containing β-CD-CQD probe and p-nitrophenol (130.0µM) results in a very small increase or slight decline in fluorescence with less than 0.1±0.05 for (I−I0)/I0; however, a sharp fluorescence enhancement is observed after the same amount of cholesterol to the others was added in the detection solution, and the value for (I−I0)/I0 is up to 2.1±0.07, indicating that this method possesses high selectivity to cholesterol. Matrix effect was further quantitatively evaluated by standard addition method using 50-fold dilution of calf serum as the matrix. Table 1 displays that original concentration of cholesterol in 50-fold dilution of calf serum is 20.8±1.3µM based on as-established method, and the recovery ratio of measured value to sum of added and original values for six samples spiked with different concentrations of cholesterol in the range of 10.0–60.0µM varies from 103±2% to 108±2%, indicating that this assay is capable to accurately quantitate cholesterol in such a complex matrix as real samples.

The specific host-guest recognition between cholesterol and β-cyclodextrin has been introduced as a new, effective assay strategy for uses in the quantification of cholesterol via a competitive interaction. The proper association between p-nitrophenol and β-cyclodextrin and strong quenching ability of p-nitrophenol to β-cyclodextrin modified carbon quantum dot nanoprobe enable the nanoprobe efficiently quenched. Stronger affinity of cholesterol than p-nitrophenol to β-cyclodextrin endows the feasibility of this strategy for quantifying cholesterol in fluorescence turn-on manner. This developed assay can achieve selective detection of cholesterol without the involvement of expensive cholesterol oxidase, in contrast to most traditional assays which require the assistance of cholesterol oxidase. The assay possesses sufficient sensitivity for real samples and can be directly applied to biological samples such as plasma. The β-CD-CQD nanoprobe-based assay is thus a significant improvement to the enzymatic assay, which is the routine detection method in clinic diagnosis. From this demonstration of the developed assay, we have further shown that host-guest recognition in supramolecular chemistry is capable of attaining high selectivity to specific targets of interest, and carbon quantum dots serve as an excellent alternative to molecular fluorophore in bioanalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 21675143 and 21405142) and Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (Grant no. LY17B050003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.02.060.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Steroidogenic enzymes: structure, function, and role in regulation of steroid hormone biosynthesis

Why is non-high-density lipoprotein cholesterol a better marker of the risk of vascular disease than low-density lipoprotein cholesterol

Determination of cholesterol and cholesterol ester with novel enzyme microsensors
